mn53:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Sakyans, at Kapilavatthu in Nigrodha’s Park.
mn53:10.1: And how, Mahānāma, is a noble disciple devoted to wakefulness?
mn53:10.2: Here, Mahānāma, during the day, while walking and sitting, a noble disciple purifies the mind [*citta*] from obstructive *dhammas* [*āvaraṇīyehi dhammehi*],
mn53:10.3: in the first watch of the night, while walking and sitting, he purifies the mind [*citta*] from obstructive *dhammas* [*āvaraṇīyehi dhammehi*],
mn53:10.4: in the middle watch of the night, he lies down on his right side in the lion’s posture, placing one foot on top of the other, with *sati*, fully aware [*sampajāno*], having placed in mind the perception [*saññā*] of rising,
mn53:10.5: in the last watch of the night, after rising, while walking and sitting, he purifies the mind [*citta*] from obstructive *dhammas* [*āvaraṇīyehi dhammehi*].
mn53:10.6: In this way, Mahānāma, a noble disciple is devoted to wakefulness.
mn53:11.1: And how, Mahānāma, is a noble disciple endowed with seven good *dhammas* [*saddhammehi*]?
mn53:11.2: Here, Mahānāma, a noble disciple has conviction; he places conviction in the Tathāgata’s awakening:
mn53:11.3: ‘So too, the *Bhagavā* is an *arahant*, perfectly awakened, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, well-gone, knower of the world, unsurpassed trainer of those who can be trained, teacher of devas and humans, awakened, the *Bhagavā*.’
mn53:12.1: He has conscience; he feels conscience regarding bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct; he feels conscience regarding falling into bad, unskillful *dhammas*.
mn53:13.1: He has moral dread; he dreads bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct; he dreads falling into bad, unskillful *dhammas*.
mn53:14.1: He is learned, remembers what he has heard, and is a store of what he has heard. Those *dhammas* that are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well phrased, which proclaim the perfectly complete and pure holy life—such *dhammas* are much heard by him, retained, recited verbally, examined by the mind, and well penetrated by view.
mn53:15.1: He dwells with aroused energy for the abandoning of unskillful *dhammas* and for the acquiring of skillful *dhammas*; strong, firm in effort, not laying down the burden regarding skillful *dhammas*.
mn53:16.1: He is with *sati* [*satimā*], endowed with the highest skill in *sati* [*satinepakkena*], able to recollect and remember what was done and said long ago.
mn53:17.1: He possesses discernment [*paññā*], endowed with discernment [*paññāya*] that goes to arising and passing away, noble, penetrative, leading to the complete ending of *dukkha*.
mn53:17.2: In this way, Mahānāma, a noble disciple is endowed with seven good *dhammas* [*saddhammehi*].
mn53:18.1: And how, Mahānāma, does a noble disciple obtain at will, without trouble, without difficulty, the four *jhānas*, which are higher-mind states and pleasant dwellings in this very life?
mn53:18.2: Here, Mahānāma, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … he enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*;
mn53:18.3: with the settling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with internal clarity … he enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*;
mn53:18.4: with the fading of *pīti* … he enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*;
mn53:18.5: with the giving up of *sukha* and the giving up of *dukkha*, and with the earlier disappearance of joy and dejection … he enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
mn53:18.6: In this way, Mahānāma, a noble disciple obtains at will, without trouble, without difficulty, the four *jhānas*, which are higher-mind states and pleasant dwellings in this very life.
mn53:19.1: When, Mahānāma, a noble disciple is thus accomplished in virtue, thus guards the doors of the indriyas, is thus moderate in eating, is thus devoted to wakefulness, is thus endowed with seven good *dhammas* [*saddhammehi*], and thus obtains at will, without trouble, without difficulty, the four *jhānas*, which are higher-mind states and pleasant dwellings in this very life, this is called a noble disciple who is a trainee in practice, who has reached maturity, capable of breaking out, capable of awakening, capable of attaining the unsurpassed security from bondage.
mn53:19.2: Just as, Mahānāma, if a hen had eight or ten or twelve eggs, and those eggs had been properly sat on by that hen, properly warmed, properly nurtured,
mn53:19.3: even if this wish did not arise in that hen: ‘Oh, may these chicks break through the shell safely with the tip of their claw or with their beak,’
mn53:19.4: still those chicks would be capable of breaking through the shell safely with the tip of their claw or with their beak.
mn53:19.5: In the same way, Mahānāma, when a noble disciple is thus accomplished in virtue, thus guards the doors of the indriyas, is thus moderate in eating, is thus devoted to wakefulness, is thus endowed with seven good *dhammas* [*saddhammehi*], and thus obtains at will, without trouble, without difficulty, the four *jhānas*, which are higher-mind states and pleasant dwellings in this very life, this is called a noble disciple who is a trainee in practice, who has reached maturity, capable of breaking out, capable of awakening, capable of attaining the unsurpassed security from bondage.
mn53:2.1: Now at that time the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu had recently had a new assembly hall built, which had not yet been used by any ascetic, brahmin, or any human being at all.
mn53:2.2: Then the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone to the *Bhagavā*, they bowed to him and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn53:2.3: “Bhante, here the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu have recently had a new assembly hall built, which has not yet been used by any ascetic, brahmin, or any human being at all.
mn53:2.4: Bhante, let the *Bhagavā* be the first to use it. When it has first been used by the *Bhagavā*, afterwards the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu will use it.
mn53:2.5: That would be for the welfare and *sukha* of the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu for a long time.”
mn53:20.1: Relying on this very unsurpassed purity of equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati* [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*], that noble disciple recollects many kinds of former lives,
mn53:20.2: that is: one birth, two births … thus with aspects and details he recollects many kinds of former lives,
mn53:20.3: this is his first breaking out, like that of a chick from the shell.
mn53:21.1: Relying on this very unsurpassed purity of equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati* [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*], that noble disciple, with the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, sees beings passing away and being reborn, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in good destinations and bad destinations … he understands beings as faring according to their *kamma*,
mn53:21.2: this is his second breaking out, like that of a chick from the shell.
mn53:22.1: Relying on this very unsurpassed purity of equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati* [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*], that noble disciple, with the destruction of the *āsavas*, dwells having realized for himself with direct knowledge, in this very life, the taintless liberation of the mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*], having attained it.
mn53:22.2: This is his third breaking out, like that of a chick from the shell.
mn53:23.1: And insofar, Mahānāma, as a noble disciple is accomplished in virtue, this belongs to his conduct;
mn53:23.2: insofar as a noble disciple guards the doors of the indriyas, this belongs to his conduct;
mn53:23.3: insofar as a noble disciple is moderate in eating, this belongs to his conduct;
mn53:23.4: insofar as a noble disciple is devoted to wakefulness, this belongs to his conduct;
mn53:23.5: insofar as a noble disciple is endowed with seven good *dhammas* [*saddhammehi*], this belongs to his conduct;
mn53:23.6: insofar as a noble disciple obtains at will, without trouble, without difficulty, the four *jhānas*, which are higher-mind states and pleasant dwellings in this very life, this belongs to his conduct.
mn53:24.1: And insofar, Mahānāma, as a noble disciple recollects many kinds of former lives, that is: one birth, two births … thus with aspects and details he recollects many kinds of former lives, this belongs to his knowledge;
mn53:24.2: and insofar as a noble disciple, with the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, sees beings passing away and being reborn, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in good destinations and bad destinations … he understands beings as faring according to their *kamma*, this belongs to his knowledge.
mn53:24.3: And insofar as a noble disciple, with the destruction of the *āsavas*, dwells having realized for himself with direct knowledge, in this very life, the taintless liberation of the mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*], having attained it, this belongs to his knowledge.
mn53:25.1: This is called a noble disciple accomplished in knowledge, and also accomplished in conduct, and also accomplished in knowledge and conduct.
mn53:25.2: And this verse was spoken by the Brahmā Sanaṅkumāra, Mahānāma:
mn53:25.3: ‘The noble warrior is best among people,
mn53:25.4: for those who reckon by lineage;
mn53:25.5: but one accomplished in knowledge and conduct,
mn53:25.6: is best among devas and humans.’
mn53:25.7: And that verse, Mahānāma, was well sung by the Brahmā Sanaṅkumāra, not poorly sung; well spoken, not poorly spoken; connected with meaning, not unconnected with meaning; approved by the *Bhagavā*.”
mn53:26.1: Then the *Bhagavā* rose up and addressed Venerable Ānanda:
mn53:26.3: It is good indeed, Ānanda, that you have spoken of the trainee’s practice to the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu.”
mn53:26.4: This is what Venerable Ānanda said.
mn53:26.6: Delighted, the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu rejoiced in Venerable Ānanda’s words.
mn53:26.7: The Trainee is completed, the third.
mn53:3.2: Then the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu, understanding that the *Bhagavā* had consented, rose from their seats, bowed to the *Bhagavā*, and, keeping him to their right, went to the new assembly hall. Having gone there, they completely spread the assembly hall with coverings, arranged the seats, set out a water pot, lit an oil lamp, and then went to the *Bhagavā*.
mn53:3.3: Having gone to the *Bhagavā*, they bowed to him and stood to one side.
mn53:3.4: Standing to one side, the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn53:3.5: “Bhante, the assembly hall has been completely spread with coverings, the seats have been arranged, the water pot has been set out, and the oil lamp has been lit.
mn53:3.6: Now is the time, bhante, whenever the *Bhagavā* thinks it is time.”
mn53:4.1: Then the *Bhagavā*, having robed himself and taking bowl and robe, went together with the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghena*] to the assembly hall. Having gone there, he washed his feet, entered the assembly hall, and sat leaning against the middle pillar, facing east.
mn53:4.2: And the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅgho*] too, having washed their feet, entered the assembly hall and sat leaning against the western wall, facing east, with the *Bhagavā* before them.
mn53:4.3: And the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu too, having washed their feet, entered the assembly hall and sat leaning against the eastern wall, facing west, with the *Bhagavā* before them.
mn53:5.1: Then the *Bhagavā*, having instructed, encouraged, stirred, and gladdened the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu for much of the night with a talk on *dhamma*, addressed Venerable Ānanda:
mn53:5.2: “Speak, Ānanda, on the trainee’s practice for the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu.
mn53:5.5: “Yes, bhante,” Venerable Ānanda replied to the *Bhagavā*.
mn53:5.6: Then the *Bhagavā*, arranging his outer robe in four folds, lay down on his right side in the lion’s posture, placing one foot on top of the other, with *sati*, fully aware [*sampajāno*], having placed in mind the perception [*saññā*] of rising.
mn53:6.1: Then Venerable Ānanda addressed Mahānāma the Sakyan:
mn53:6.2: “Here, Mahānāma, a noble disciple is accomplished in virtue, guards the doors of the indriyas, is moderate in eating, is devoted to wakefulness, is endowed with seven good *dhammas* [*saddhammehi*], and obtains at will, without trouble, without difficulty, the four *jhānas*, which are higher-mind states and pleasant dwellings in this very life.
mn53:7.1: And how, Mahānāma, is a noble disciple accomplished in virtue?
mn53:7.2: Here, Mahānāma, a noble disciple is virtuous, dwelling restrained by the restraint of the monastic code, accomplished in conduct and resort, seeing danger in the slightest faults, and having undertaken the training rules, trains in them.
mn53:7.3: In this way, Mahānāma, a noble disciple is accomplished in virtue.
mn53:8.1: And how, Mahānāma, does a noble disciple guard the doors of the indriyas?
mn53:8.10: In this way, Mahānāma, a noble disciple guards the doors of the indriyas.
mn53:8.2: Here, Mahānāma, when a noble disciple sees a form with the eye, he does not grasp at a *nimitta* or at secondary details.
mn53:8.3: Since, if he were to dwell with the eye indriya unguarded, bad, unskillful *dhammas* of desire and dejection might invade him, he practices for its restraint, he guards the eye indriya, he undertakes restraint of the eye indriya.
mn53:8.4: When hearing a sound with the ear …
mn53:8.5: when smelling an odor with the nose …
mn53:8.6: when tasting a flavor with the tongue …
mn53:8.7: when touching a tangible object with the body …
mn53:8.8: when cognizing a *dhamma* with the mind, he does not grasp at a *nimitta* or at secondary details.
mn53:8.9: Since, if he were to dwell with the mind indriya unguarded, bad, unskillful *dhammas* of desire and dejection might invade him, he practices for its restraint, he guards the mind indriya, he undertakes restraint of the mind indriya.
mn53:9.1: And how, Mahānāma, is a noble disciple moderate in eating?
mn53:9.2: Here, Mahānāma, a noble disciple, reflecting appropriately, takes food:
mn53:9.3: ‘Not for amusement, not for intoxication, not for fattening, not for adornment, but only for the maintenance and continuance of this body, for ending distress, and for supporting the holy life. Thus I will allay old *vedanā* and not produce new *vedanā*, and I will live blamelessly and at ease.’
mn53:9.4: In this way, Mahānāma, a noble disciple is moderate in eating.
